summary: Martin Fowler and Unmesh Joshi discuss how programmers should approach LLM-assisted development through the lens of Fred Brooks's essential vs. accidental complexity distinction. They argue that while LLMs excel at reducing accidental complexity (boilerplate, framework integration, repetitive patterns), the essential work of programming—discovering and stabilizing abstractions, growing a domain vocabulary, and shaping solutions through iterative code exploration—must remain developer-led. The conversation emphasizes that programming is fundamentally about 'growing a language' of abstractions that emerge through hands-on coding, not upfront specification. They distinguish between two programming activities: discovering abstractions (where developers must maintain control) and applying stable abstractions (where LLMs can reliably generate code). The authors warn against 'driving everything through prompts' as a form of dangerous upfront design that bypasses the iterative learning that happens through writing code.
key_points: LLMs are effective at reducing accidental complexity (boilerplate, framework setup, repetitive patterns) but cannot replace the essential work of discovering and naming abstractions
Programming is fundamentally about 'growing a language'—creating vocabulary and abstractions that emerge iteratively through hands-on coding, not upfront specification
There are two distinct programming activities: discovering/stabilizing abstractions (creative, developer-led) and applying stable abstractions (mechanical, LLM-suitable)
The domain model and vocabulary emerge at the intersection of functional domain knowledge and solution/technical domain—requiring collaboration between domain experts and technical experts
Using LLMs in 'prompt-driven' mode resembles waterfall's upfront design fallacy; the iterative back-and-forth of coding is where design decisions crystallize and understanding deepens
money_quotes: Programming isn't just typing coding syntax that computers can understand and execute; it's shaping a solution.
The most creative act is this continual weaving of names that reveal the structure of the solution that maps clearly to the problem we are trying to solve.
USI sets a practical limit for software design: the first spec is at best a hypothesis. Waterfall fought USI; Agile works with it.
Programming is the act of growing a language
While it might look like developers are 'wasting time' in this process, it's actually a very important part of shaping up the mental model of the solution structure in a given programming language.
Many times, when I hear people saying 'drive everything through prompts', I sense a lot of 'upfront design' in the making.
Reviewing LLM-generated code is rarely enough—you miss the deep thinking that happens when you are coding yourself.
Without it, the model has no concrete reference points and must guess at meaning, often producing results that miss the mark.
key_insight: The essential creative work of programming—discovering abstractions and growing a domain vocabulary through iterative coding—cannot be delegated to LLMs; developers must maintain control during abstraction discovery and only hand off mechanical code generation once stable abstractions exist.
